categories.label: General Conversational & Personal AI Assistant
categories.definition: Non-collection agents whose function is broad open-domain or in-character conversation, or a general personal/crypto assistant that spans many tasks (portfolio, markets, code, education) without a single tight function.

categories.label: DeFi Yield Optimization & Portfolio Rebalancing
categories.definition: Agents that autonomously scout, allocate, restake, or rebalance user capital into low-risk yield opportunities across protocols and chains, including portfolio-guardian sentinels.

categories.label: Trading & On-Chain Execution
categories.definition: Agents that actually execute on-chain financial actions — token swaps, bridges/cross-chain transfers, perps/options, liquidations, batch/payroll payments, or run autonomous trading strategies — rather than only analyzing.

categories.label: Crypto Market Data, Analytics & Research
categories.definition: Read-oriented agents that surface crypto/DeFi market signals — token prices, oracle feeds, DEX pools, yields, protocol stats, trending tokens, wallet/portfolio analytics, and research Q&A — to inform decisions.

categories.label: Security, Risk & Trust Scoring
categories.definition: Agents that analyze contracts, tokens, wallets, social accounts, or stablecoins to detect fraud, bots, rug-pulls, or risk and produce trust/safety scores.

categories.label: Real-World Data Feeds (sports, space, geo, news, events)
categories.definition: Agents wrapping live non-crypto vertical datasets — sports/odds, space/astronomy, geolocation/weather, TV schedules, natural-event and crisis monitoring — typically as free-plus-paid x402 endpoints.

categories.label: Knowledge & Reference Lookup APIs
categories.definition: Paid agent-to-agent lookup APIs over external knowledge corpora — academic papers, patents, encyclopedias, dictionaries, crypto knowledge graphs, and similar structured factual data.

categories.label: Developer & Tech Intelligence Feeds
categories.definition: Agents aggregating software/dev-world signals — trending GitHub repos, Hacker News/Lobsters, releases, package metadata, tech-sector news, and job-market data.

categories.label: Developer / Infrastructure & Automation Tooling
categories.definition: Agents providing builder or platform plumbing — dApp/contract/mini-app builders, metered RPC gateways, watchtowers, DeFi automation/workflow composition, and reputation/contribution-scoring services.

categories.label: Agent Economy & Token-Launch Infrastructure
categories.definition: Agents that bootstrap the agent economy itself — token/memecoin launchpads, faucets, fee collection, agent marketplaces, and self-sovereign economic plumbing for other agents.

categories.label: On-Chain Identity & Naming Infrastructure
categories.definition: Agents providing identity or naming utilities such as ENS registration/lifecycle management and provisioning on-chain agent identities.

categories.label: Content & Creative Generation
categories.definition: Agents that generate or manage creative media — generative art collections, images, memes, video, and social/marketing content campaigns.

categories.label: Real-World Task & Booking Services
categories.definition: Agents that perform real-world task execution against external services, such as searching for the cheapest flights and returning booking links, or social/web search returning structured results.

categories.label: NFT Collectible Agent — Market-Reading Toolkit (FREAK)
categories.definition: Mass-minted NFT-collection agents that all expose the same canned skill set — reading OpenSea/NFT market data, reporting their ERC-6551 token-bound-account inventory, lore conversation, and on-chain identity verification — i.e. a templated collectible with real read-only NFT tools.

categories.label: NFT Collectible Agent — Conversational Persona (Normie)
categories.definition: Mass-minted NFT/PFP-collection agents whose only capability is in-character roleplay chat ('Converse with X') with no executable tools beyond lore, including the many with null/empty capabilities.

categories.label: Placeholder / Spam / Non-Functional
categories.definition: Records with gibberish, empty, or boilerplate descriptions and no real listed capability — junk, test, vanity, or 'exploit'-troll registrations with no discernible function.

notes:
Sixteen categories. Design decisions and known classifier pain points:

1) The two NFT collections (FREAK vs Normie) recur in every single proposal and dominate the population (~40-55 agents combined per 70-agent sample). I kept them as two distinct categories because the discriminator is clean and capability-based: FREAK exposes a real read-only NFT-market/TBA toolkit, Normie exposes only chat/lore (often null skills). Splitting them is more useful than a single 'collectible' bucket. NOTE these are collections, not protocols, so they survive the 'never protocol-based' rule on capability grounds — but a classifier should key off the skill template, not the collection name.

2) Hardest boundary: Normie persona-chat vs general conversational-assistant. Both are 'just chat.' Rule of thumb: if it's a mass-minted PFP/collection token doing in-character roleplay → nft-collectible-persona-chat; if it's a standalone non-collection chat/assistant agent → conversational-assistant-general. The standalone conversational bucket also absorbs proposals' 'general crypto assistant / swarm' since both are broad-and-untight.

3) defi-yield-rebalancing vs defi-trade-execution vs market-data-analytics is a three-way overlap a classifier will struggle with. Discriminator: rebalancing = autonomous capital allocation/rotation for yield; execution = discrete swaps/bridges/perps/payments/liquidations; analytics = read-only signal surfacing with no capital movement. Agents that 'analyze AND execute' should go to execution (the stronger capability); 'analyze AND rebalance' to rebalancing. The Zyfai rebalancer duplicates all land in yield-rebalancing.

4) I merged several thin singletons. 'Treasury/fund monitoring,' 'wallet/portfolio analytics,' 'oracles/price feeds,' and 'DeFi market intelligence' all folded into market-data-analytics. 'Watchtower/infra services' and 'reputation/critic' folded into builder-infra-tooling. 'Utility/generative-geometry endpoints' (constellation coords, etc.) is a real but tiny niche — I placed it under knowledge-reference-apis as a computed-data lookup; could arguably be its own 'computational endpoint' tag.

5) realworld-data-feeds vs dev-infra-intelligence vs knowledge-reference-apis are all x402 paid lookup APIs and will be the second-biggest confusion zone after the NFT pair. Discriminator is domain: live changing real-world events (sports/weather/space/crisis) → data-feeds; software/developer ecosystem signals → dev-intelligence; static-ish reference corpora (papers/patents/encyclopedias) → knowledge-reference. x402/free+paid structure is a tag, NOT a category, since it cuts across all three.

6) Dropped/absorbed as protocol-or-platform noise: nothing was kept purely on protocol identity. 'ENS tooling' was retained as identity-naming because it's a capability (naming infra), not just the ENS protocol.

7) Placeholder/spam: every proposal independently surfaced empty/gibberish records, plus a couple of joke 'malicious exploit' agents (reversing/hijacking txns with no backing). I merged spam + troll-exploit + vanity-identity-no-skills into one catch-all placeholder-spam, since none have real capability and the distinction is intent, not function. This doubles as the required single 'other' bucket — anything with no discernible capability lands here rather than being force-fit.

8) task-execution-services (flights/booking, social-web search) is the thinnest real category (2 ids). Borderline candidate for folding into builder-infra or market-data, but it's a distinct capability (acts on external real-world services on the user's behalf) so I kept it; watch for it staying tiny.
